answer:
Comparing Two Microservices Boundary Approaches with Trade-offs

1. Definition & Context

Microservices boundaries define how you split a system into independently deployable services. The two main approaches are Bounded Context (Domain-Driven) — splitting along business domain boundaries (e.g., Leave Management, Notifications, Employee Directory) — and Technical Layer Split — splitting by technical function (e.g., API layer, business logic layer, data access layer). Domain-driven boundaries are generally preferred because they align service ownership with business capabilities, but technical splits can be simpler for small teams.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the initial monolith had everything in one Fastify app — leave CRUD, approval chain logic, notification sending, employee lookup, and reporting. As the system grew, Manoj evaluated two boundary strategies:

Domain-Driven (chosen): Split into Leave Service (leave CRUD + business rules), Approval Service (3-level chain + escalation), and Notification Service (Socket.io + email). Each service owned its data and exposed well-defined APIs. The Leave Service didn't need to know HOW notifications were sent — it just published a leave.status.changed event.

Technical Layer (rejected): Would have created an API Gateway layer, a Business Logic layer, and a Data Access layer. Manoj rejected this because it meant every feature change (e.g., adding a new leave type) would require changes across all three layers, defeating the purpose of independent deployability.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// Domain-Driven Boundary — Leave Service (self-contained)
// leave-service/src/routes.ts
import { FastifyInstance } from 'fastify';
import { prisma } from './db'; // Leave service's own Prisma client
import { publishEvent } from './events';

export async function leaveRoutes(app: FastifyInstance) {
  // Leave service owns ALL leave-related logic
  app.post('/leave/apply', async (req, reply) => {
    const { startDate, endDate, type, reason } = req.body as any;
    const userId = req.headers['x-user-id'] as string;

    // Business rule: no weekends, no backdating
    const start = new Date(startDate);
    if (start < new Date()) {
      return reply.code(400).send({ error: 'Cannot backdate leave' });
    }

    const days = calculateBusinessDays(start, new Date(endDate));

    // Check balance (leave service owns balance data)
    const balance = await prisma.leaveBalance.findUnique({
      where: { employeeId_type: { employeeId: userId, type } },
    });
    if (!balance || balance.remaining < days) {
      return reply.code(400).send({ error: 'Insufficient balance' });
    }

    const leave = await prisma.leaveRequest.create({
      data: {
        employeeId: userId, startDate: start,
        endDate: new Date(endDate), type, reason,
        days, status: 'submitted',
      },
    });

    // Publish event — Approval Service will pick it up
    await publishEvent('leave.submitted', {
      leaveId: leave.id,
      employeeId: userId,
      days,
    });

    return reply.code(201).send(leave);
  });
}

// Approval Service listens for leave events
// approval-service/src/listeners.ts
import { subscribeEvent } from './events';
import { prisma } from './db'; // Approval service's own DB

subscribeEvent('leave.submitted', async (data) => {
  // Look up approval chain for this employee
  const chain = await prisma.approvalChain.findMany({
    where: { employeeId: data.employeeId },
    orderBy: { level: 'asc' },
  });

  // Create approval task for L1 manager
  await prisma.approvalTask.create({
    data: {
      leaveId: data.leaveId,
      approverId: chain[0].managerId,
      level: 1,
      status: 'pending',
    },
  });

  // Notify the manager (via Notification Service)
  await publishEvent('approval.pending', {
    approverId: chain[0].managerId,
    leaveId: data.leaveId,
    employeeId: data.employeeId,
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

When splitting the monolith, Manoj hit a problem: the Leave Service needed employee names for response payloads, but employee data now lived in a separate Employee Service (or remained in the main app). Querying across services for every leave list request added 200ms latency.

Manoj solved it by implementing data denormalization — when an employee was created or updated, the Employee Service published an employee.updated event. The Leave Service subscribed and stored a lightweight employee_cache table with just id, name, department. This eliminated cross-service queries for the common case. The cache was eventually consistent (updates propagated within seconds), which was acceptable for display names.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Cross-service joins become expensiveDenormalize frequently-needed data; use events to keep local caches updated; accept eventual consistency for non-critical fields
Distributed transactions across service boundariesUse Saga pattern — each service handles its step and publishes success/failure events; implement compensating transactions for rollback
Service boundaries drawn too granularly (nano-services)Start with a modular monolith; only extract a service when deployment independence is actually needed; merge back services that always deploy together

7. Validation Strategy

  • Independence test: Deploy Leave Service alone without Approval Service — verify leave creation works (event is queued, not lost)
  • Contract test: Use Pact to verify Leave Service and Approval Service agree on event schema
  • Latency test: End-to-end leave application flow should complete in < 500ms despite crossing service boundaries
  • Data consistency test: Update employee name, verify Leave Service's local cache reflects change within 5 seconds

Designing a Practical Microservices Boundary Implementation

1. Definition & Context

Designing microservices boundaries for a mid-size product requires identifying the right seams — places where the system can be split with minimal cross-service communication. The practical approach is to start with a modular monolith (clear module boundaries within one deployable) and extract services only when there's a concrete scaling, deployment, or team-ownership reason. Premature splitting creates distributed monolith complexity without the benefits.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

Manoj's HR Leave System started as a Fastify monolith with 12 REST endpoints. As the team grew from 1 to 3 developers, they needed clear ownership boundaries. Manoj implemented a modular monolith first — organizing code into modules/leave, modules/approval, modules/notification, and modules/employee. Each module had its own routes, services, and Prisma schema. Modules communicated through a shared EventEmitter, NOT by importing each other's internal functions.

After 3 months, the Notification module was extracted into a separate service because: (1) Socket.io needed its own scaling (websocket connections are long-lived), (2) email sending was slow and shouldn't block leave CRUD, and (3) a different developer specialized in real-time systems wanted to own it independently.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// Phase 1: Modular Monolith — Clear boundaries within one app
// src/modules/leave/index.ts
import { FastifyInstance } from 'fastify';
import { leaveRoutes } from './routes';
import { leaveListeners } from './listeners';
import { leaveEvents } from '../../shared/events';

export async function leaveModule(app: FastifyInstance) {
  // Register this module's routes
  app.register(leaveRoutes, { prefix: '/api/leave' });

  // Register event listeners
  leaveListeners(leaveEvents);

  app.log.info('Leave module loaded');
}

// src/modules/leave/routes.ts — ONLY imports from within leave module
import { FastifyInstance } from 'fastify';
import { LeaveService } from './service';

export async function leaveRoutes(app: FastifyInstance) {
  const service = new LeaveService();

  app.post('/apply', async (req, reply) => {
    const result = await service.applyLeave(
      req.headers['x-user-id'] as string,
      req.body as any
    );
    return reply.code(201).send(result);
  });
}

// Boundary enforcement — lint rule to prevent cross-module imports
// .eslintrc.js
module.exports = {
  rules: {
    'no-restricted-imports': ['error', {
      patterns: [
        {
          group: ['../approval/*', '../notification/*', '../employee/*'],
          message: 'Cross-module imports are forbidden. Use events instead.',
        },
      ],
    }],
  },
};

// Phase 2: Extract Notification Service
// notification-service/src/index.ts
import Fastify from 'fastify';
import { Server } from 'socket.io';
import Redis from 'ioredis';

const app = Fastify();
const io = new Server(app.server, { cors: { origin: '*' } });

// Subscribe to events from main app via Redis
const sub = new Redis(process.env.REDIS_URL!);
sub.subscribe('leave.approved', 'leave.submitted', 'approval.pending');

sub.on('message', async (channel, message) => {
  const data = JSON.parse(message);
  switch (channel) {
    case 'leave.approved':
      io.to(`user:${data.employeeId}`).emit('notification', {
        type: 'leave-approved',
        message: `Your leave was approved!`,
      });
      await sendEmail(data.employeeId, 'Leave Approved', data);
      break;
    case 'approval.pending':
      io.to(`user:${data.approverId}`).emit('notification', {
        type: 'approval-needed',
        message: `New leave request pending your approval`,
      });
      break;
  }
});

app.listen({ port: 3001 });

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

After extracting the Notification Service, Manoj noticed that some notifications were delivered twice. The root cause: during the migration, the monolith still had the old EventEmitter-based notification listener registered, AND the new Redis-based Notification Service was also listening. Both handled the same events.

Manoj found this by checking listener counts in the monolith (leaveEvents.listenerCount('leave.approved') returned 2 — one for the old notification handler and one for the Redis publisher). The fix was simple but easy to miss: remove the old notification listener from the monolith after the service extraction. He added a migration checklist item: "After extracting a module, grep for all old listener registrations and remove them."

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Extracting too early — adding distributed system complexity without benefitRule: only extract when you have a concrete deployment, scaling, or ownership reason; document the reason in an ADR (Architecture Decision Record)
Shared database between services — creating hidden couplingEach service must own its schema; use database-per-service with events for data sync; enforce at the Prisma schema level
Old listeners remaining after service extraction (duplicate processing)Migration checklist: search for all event listeners and remove duplicates; add integration tests that count event deliveries

7. Validation Strategy

  • Boundary enforcement: ESLint rule prevents cross-module imports — CI fails if any module imports another module's internals
  • Independent deployment test: Deploy Notification Service independently, verify notifications still work (events flow via Redis)
  • Duplicate delivery test: Send one event, verify exactly one notification is delivered (not zero, not two)
  • Module health check: Each module/service exposes /health with dependency status (DB connected, Redis connected)

Designing a Practical Monolith Modularization Implementation

1. Definition & Context

Monolith Modularization organizes a monolithic application into well-bounded modules that could potentially become microservices later, without actually splitting them into separate deployables. Each module has its own routes, business logic, data access, and clear interfaces. Modules communicate via events or defined contracts, NOT by importing each other's internal implementation. This gives you the simplicity of a single deployment with the organizational clarity of microservices.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the initial codebase had a flat structure — all routes in src/routes/, all services in src/services/, all models in src/models/. A change to the leave approval flow required touching files across 4 directories. Manoj reorganized into vertical modules:

src/
  modules/
    leave/       → routes.ts, service.ts, schema.prisma, types.ts
    approval/    → routes.ts, service.ts, schema.prisma, types.ts
    notification/→ routes.ts, service.ts, types.ts
    employee/    → routes.ts, service.ts, schema.prisma, types.ts
  shared/
    events.ts    → EventEmitter (inter-module communication)
    auth.ts      → JWT middleware (shared)
    types.ts     → Shared TypeScript interfaces

Each module could be understood, modified, and tested independently. The leave module didn't know how notifications were implemented — it just emitted leave.submitted and the notification module handled it.

3. Module Structure Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// src/app.ts — Modular registration
import Fastify from 'fastify';
import { leaveModule } from './modules/leave';
import { approvalModule } from './modules/approval';
import { notificationModule } from './modules/notification';
import { employeeModule } from './modules/employee';

const app = Fastify({ logger: true });

// Each module is self-contained — registers its own routes + listeners
app.register(leaveModule);
app.register(approvalModule);
app.register(notificationModule);
app.register(employeeModule);

app.listen({ port: 3000 });

// src/modules/leave/service.ts — Module's business logic
import { prisma } from '../../shared/db';
import { appEvents } from '../../shared/events';
import type { ApplyLeaveDTO, LeaveResponse } from './types';

export class LeaveService {
  async applyLeave(userId: string, dto: ApplyLeaveDTO): Promise {
    // Validate business rules
    this.validateDates(dto.startDate, dto.endDate);
    this.validateNoOverlap(userId, dto.startDate, dto.endDate);

    const days = this.calculateBusinessDays(dto.startDate, dto.endDate);

    const leave = await prisma.leaveRequest.create({
      data: {
        employeeId: userId,
        startDate: dto.startDate,
        endDate: dto.endDate,
        type: dto.type,
        reason: dto.reason,
        days,
        status: 'submitted',
      },
    });

    // Communicate via events — NOT by importing approval module
    appEvents.emit('leave.submitted', {
      leaveId: leave.id,
      employeeId: userId,
      days,
      type: dto.type,
    });

    return leave;
  }

  private validateDates(start: Date, end: Date): void {
    if (start < new Date()) throw new Error('Cannot backdate');
    if (end < start) throw new Error('End before start');
    if (start.getDay() === 0 || start.getDay() === 6)
      throw new Error('Cannot start on weekend');
  }

  private validateNoOverlap(userId: string, start: Date, end: Date) {
    // Check for overlapping approved leaves
  }

  private calculateBusinessDays(start: Date, end: Date): number {
    let count = 0;
    const cur = new Date(start);
    while (cur <= end) {
      if (cur.getDay() !== 0 && cur.getDay() !== 6) count++;
      cur.setDate(cur.getDate() + 1);
    }
    return count;
  }
}

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

During modularization, Manoj discovered a circular dependency: the Leave module imported a helper from the Approval module to check if the current user was a manager, and the Approval module imported from Leave to get leave details. Node.js handled this at runtime (partial module loading), but it caused unpredictable behavior — sometimes the import was undefined.

Manoj diagnosed it by adding console.log at the top of each module file to track load order. He broke the cycle by extracting the shared isManager check into the shared/auth.ts module and making the Approval module subscribe to leave events instead of importing leave functions directly. He also added an ESLint rule to prevent future cross-module imports.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Circular dependencies between modulesUse events for inter-module communication; extract shared logic to shared/; add ESLint no-restricted-imports rule
Shared database creates hidden coupling (module A reads module B's tables directly)Each module should only access its own Prisma models; use events to propagate data between modules
Module boundaries erode over time as developers take shortcutsCI check: run ESLint import restriction rules; code review checklist item: "Does this PR cross module boundaries?"

7. Validation Strategy

  • Boundary test: ESLint CI job verifies no cross-module imports exist in codebase
  • Module isolation test: Disable one module (don't register it), verify the app still starts and other modules work (events are emitted but no listener — that's fine)
  • Circular dependency check: Use madge --circular src/ in CI to detect import cycles
  • Integration test per module: Each module has its own test suite that can run independently

Debugging a Real Monolith Modularization Issue

1. Definition & Context

Debugging modularization issues typically involves three categories: boundary violations (modules importing each other's internals), event wiring bugs (events emitted but no listener registered, or duplicate listeners), and shared state conflicts (modules accidentally sharing database connections, caches, or global variables). In an interview, you demonstrate this by walking through a real incident where module boundaries caused unexpected behavior.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, after modularizing, a bug appeared where the leave list API returned employee data that was 24 hours stale. The Leave module stored a local copy of employee names (for display in leave list responses), but the Employee module's update endpoint didn't notify the Leave module about name changes. The employee data in leave responses was stale until the next leave request was created (which fetched fresh data).

3. Diagnostic Flow Diagram

4. Debugging Code

// BEFORE — Employee module update (no event)
// modules/employee/service.ts
async updateEmployee(id: string, data: UpdateEmployeeDTO) {
  const employee = await prisma.employee.update({
    where: { id },
    data: { name: data.name, department: data.department },
  });
  return employee; // No event emitted!
}

// Leave module stored employee name at creation time
// modules/leave/service.ts
async applyLeave(userId: string, dto: ApplyLeaveDTO) {
  const employee = await prisma.employee.findUnique({ where: { id: userId } });
  const leave = await prisma.leaveRequest.create({
    data: {
      ...dto,
      employeeId: userId,
      employeeName: employee!.name, // Denormalized — becomes stale!
    },
  });
}

// AFTER — Employee module emits event on update
// modules/employee/service.ts
import { appEvents } from '../../shared/events';

async updateEmployee(id: string, data: UpdateEmployeeDTO) {
  const employee = await prisma.employee.update({
    where: { id },
    data: { name: data.name, department: data.department },
  });

  // Emit event so other modules can react
  appEvents.emit('employee.updated', {
    employeeId: id,
    name: employee.name,
    department: employee.department,
  });

  return employee;
}

// Leave module subscribes and updates denormalized data
// modules/leave/listeners.ts
import { appEvents } from '../../shared/events';
import { prisma } from '../../shared/db';

export function leaveListeners() {
  appEvents.on('employee.updated', async (data) => {
    // Update all leave records with new employee name
    await prisma.leaveRequest.updateMany({
      where: { employeeId: data.employeeId },
      data: { employeeName: data.name },
    });
  });
}

// Test: verify event propagation
import { test } from 'node:test';
import assert from 'node:assert';

test('employee name update propagates to leave records', async () => {
  // Create a leave with old name
  await prisma.leaveRequest.create({
    data: { employeeId: 'emp-1', employeeName: 'Old Name', ... },
  });

  // Update employee name (triggers event)
  await employeeService.updateEmployee('emp-1', { name: 'New Name' });

  // Wait for async listener
  await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 100));

  // Verify leave record updated
  const leave = await prisma.leaveRequest.findFirst({
    where: { employeeId: 'emp-1' },
  });
  assert.strictEqual(leave!.employeeName, 'New Name');
});

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj noticed this bug when HR updated an employee's name after marriage, but the leave history page still showed the maiden name. He traced the data flow:

  1. Checked the Leave API response — employeeName field was the old name.
  2. Checked the leave_requests table directly — confirmed the employeeName column had the old value.
  3. Checked the employees table — name was already updated to the new value.
  4. Searched for employee.updated in the codebase — zero results. The Employee module didn't emit any events.
  5. Added the event emission to Employee module + listener in Leave module. The fix took 15 minutes, but the key insight was: every module that denormalizes data from another module MUST subscribe to updates from that module.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Denormalized data becomes stale when source module doesn't emit eventsEstablish a rule: any module that writes data must emit an event for that write. Code review checks for missing event emissions
Updating denormalized data in bulk (updateMany) can be slowQueue the update as a background job; for display-only fields, eventual consistency within 1 minute is acceptable
Event listener fails silently — denormalized data stays stale foreverAdd a reconciliation job that periodically compares denormalized fields against the source of truth and fixes discrepancies

7. Validation Strategy

  • Propagation test: Update employee name, query leave list — verify new name appears within 1 second
  • Event coverage audit: Script that lists all prisma.*.update calls and checks each has a corresponding event emission
  • Reconciliation test: Manually corrupt a denormalized field, run reconciliation job, verify it's corrected
  • Playwright E2E: Update employee profile → navigate to leave history → verify new name displays
